food_subgroup: Liquid milk — fresh whole cow's milk

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 100 mL serving of fresh whole cow's milk, approximately 103 g, as offered as an add-on topping at the restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Milk, whole, 3.25% milkfat, with added vitamin D — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 746782)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy whole cow's milk (3.25% fat) is the standard reference for fresh whole milk per 100 g. Values are representative of pasteurised full-cream cow's milk as served in Singapore food-service contexts. HPB Singapore Foodo does not carry a standalone fresh milk entry; USDA SR Legacy is the highest-priority approved secondary source for this staple dairy ingredient.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Whole cow's milk GI = 31 (low) is a well-established value from the University of Sydney GI Database and confirmed in the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables published in Diabetes Care. Glycemic load per 100 mL serving (103 g, ~4.8 g carbohydrate available) = (31 × 4.95) / 100 ≈ 1.5, classified low.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (43 mg/100 g); beneficial potassium and calcium
hypertension.guidance: Fresh whole milk contains only 43 mg sodium per 100 mL and provides potassium (150 mg) and calcium (113 mg), both of which support blood-pressure regulation; a 100 mL serving is unlikely to raise blood pressure and can be included in a DASH-style dietary pattern for hypertensive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 31) and modest lactose-derived carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of 31 and a glycemic load of approximately 1.5 per 100 mL serving, fresh whole milk produces a minimal postprandial glucose response and is generally considered safe for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate amounts as part of a balanced diet.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (1.87 g/100 g) and dietary cholesterol (10 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fresh whole milk contributes saturated fat (1.87 g per 100 mL) which may modestly raise LDL cholesterol; individuals with established cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia should consider limiting intake or substituting with reduced-fat milk, while noting that dairy-derived nutrients such as calcium and potassium confer cardiovascular benefits at moderate consumption levels.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fresh whole cow's milk is a minimally processed dairy beverage obtained by pasteurising raw cow's milk without removing the natural fat fraction (approximately 3.25% fat). It has a smooth, creamy texture and a mildly sweet flavour derived from lactose. In Singapore food-service contexts, fresh milk is commonly offered as an add-on topping or accompaniment to Chinese-style dessert drinks and tang shui (糖水) beverages, enhancing richness and mouthfeel. Nutritionally, a 100 mL serving provides approximately 61 kcal, 3.15 g protein, 3.25 g fat, and 4.8 g carbohydrate, along with meaningful amounts of calcium (113 mg), potassium (150 mg), riboflavin (0.169 mg), and vitamin B12 (0.45 mcg). The glycemic index of whole milk is low (GI ≈ 31), making it suitable for glycaemic management. Milk is a source of high-quality complete protein containing all essential amino acids. It is a significant allergen for individuals with cow's milk protein allergy or lactose intolerance. Clinically, fresh whole milk is appropriate for most healthy adults in moderate quantities; those managing cardiovascular disease should monitor saturated fat intake, while individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can generally include it given its low GI and glycaemic load.

Is fresh milk good for diabetics?
What is the GI of whole milk?
Does fresh milk raise blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for whole cow's milk (FDC ID 746782), which is an exact match for fresh whole cow's milk. GI value of 31 is a well-established figure from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from this authoritative source.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; milk contains no dietary fibre by nature (naturally zero). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for whole pasteurised milk; trace amounts are destroyed during pasteurisation (confirmed zero). monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 sentinel NOT used; value 0.81 g is reported. trans_fat_g value 0.07 g represents naturally occurring ruminant trans fats (vaccenic acid) as reported by USDA. The food is listed under cuisine 'Chinese' in the source field survey record because it is served as a topping at a Chinese dessert shop (tang shui); the food itself is a universal dairy staple. Serving size of 103 g is based on the standard density of whole milk (~1.03 g/mL) applied to the 100 mL commercial portion. No -1 sentinel values remain undocumented in per_100g; all fields are populated with USDA-reported values. GI source: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
